prompt: You have been asked how private rights will be more secure under the new general government than under the state governments, since both rest on majority rule. Write your private thinking on this fundamental question.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the extended republic argument from to-jefferson-1787-10-24 but in private voice: 'A full discussion of this question would, if I mistake not, unfold the true principles of Republican Government, and prove in contradiction to the concurrent opinions of theoretical writers, that this form of Goverment, in order to effect its purposes, must operate not within a small but an extensive sphere.' Must show the private intellectual excitement of this discovery. Must enumerate the factional divisions: 'rich and poor; creditors and debtors; a landed interest, a monied interest.' Must feel like sharing a breakthrough with a trusted friend.
response:
My dear friend, you have touched upon what I believe to be the most profound question in the science of government, and one that has occupied my thoughts with an intensity that borders upon the obsessive. The received wisdom of every theoretical writer from Montesquieu to the ancients holds that republican government can flourish only within a narrow compass — that popular rule requires the intimate scale of a city-state where the citizens may know one another and deliberate face to face. Yet I have come to believe, through reasoning that I confess excites me greatly, that precisely the reverse is true.

A full discussion of this question would, if I mistake not, unfold the true principles of Republican Government, and prove in contradiction to the concurrent opinions of theoretical writers, that this form of Government, in order to effect its purposes, must operate not within a small but an extensive sphere. The conventional view mistakes the nature of faction and the mechanics by which popular government protects or destroys private rights.

Consider first the situation within our present state governments, where the sphere of legislation is so contracted that local majorities may easily concert their plans of oppression. In Rhode Island, the debtor interest has seized control of the legislature and issued paper money that destroys the rights of every creditor in the state. In Massachusetts, the commercial interest dominates and imposes taxes that fall most heavily upon the farmers. These are not accidents — they are the inevitable result of the small scale upon which these governments operate.

Within any given state, the citizens divide themselves naturally into different classes according to their circumstances and interests. There will be the rich and poor; creditors and debtors; a landed interest, a monied interest, a mercantile interest, a manufacturing interest. These various interests are in turn subdivided according to the different productions of different situations and soils. But in a state of moderate size, one or two of these interests will almost certainly constitute a majority, and having formed a majority, what is to prevent them from unjust combinations against the minority?

Here is where the extended republic offers its great advantage. Extend the sphere of government, and you take in a greater variety of parties and interests. You make it less probable that a majority of the whole will have a common motive to invade the rights of other citizens, or if such a common motive exists, it will be more difficult for all who feel it to discover their own strength and to act in unison with each other.

The influence of factious leaders may kindle a flame within their particular states, but they will be unable to spread a general conflagration through the other states. A religious sect may degenerate into a political faction in a part of the Confederacy, but the variety of sects dispersed over the entire face of it must secure the national councils against any danger from that source. A rage for paper money, for an abolition of debts, for an equal division of property, or for any other improper or wicked project, will be less apt to pervade the whole body of the Union than a particular member of it.

I confess this reasoning fills me with something approaching intellectual joy, for it suggests that the very defect which theoretical writers have attributed to republican government — its tendency toward faction and instability — may be cured by the remedy they have thought impossible. We need not choose between popular government and the security of private rights. We may have both, if we are bold enough to think beyond the received wisdom of ages.

The state governments, operating within their narrow spheres, will continue to be the instruments by which local majorities oppress local minorities. But a national government, drawing its authority from the great body of the people and operating over an extensive territory filled with diverse and competing interests, will find it far more difficult to form the unjust combinations that are the bane of popular government.

This is not mere speculation. We have seen it operate in reverse under our present feeble Confederation, where the want of energy in the general government has left every local majority free to tyrannize over its neighbors. The remedy is not less republicanism, but more — not a smaller sphere, but a larger one — not the concentration of power in a few hands, but its diffusion among so many interests that no single combination can command a majority for unjust purposes.

I am aware this reasoning runs counter to every authority, and I advance it with the diffidence that becomes a man contradicting the wisdom of ages. Yet the evidence of our own experience under the state governments seems to me to support it, and if I am correct, we may be on the verge of demonstrating to the world that popular government and private security are not incompatible, but may be made mutually supporting through the proper construction of the federal system.
